Bash(ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 chmod:*).risk: Pre-allowing the ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 envelope around chmod could in principle let a malicious or buggy automation set chmod 777/666/000 on repo files (world-writable or fully locked) without the usual Claude Code permission prompt.
Bash(ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 chmod:*).rationale: Two layers of protection remain in place: (a) guard-workspace.sh blocks any chmod with absolute paths outside ~/projects/ (with /tmp, /var/folders, /dev/null|stdin|stdout|stderr whitelisted); (b) guard-destructive.sh blocks chmod 777|666|000 unless ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 is the operator-typed prefix. Pre-allowing 'ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 chmod:*' in settings.json removes the redundant Claude Code prompt when the operator has already explicitly opted into the bypass envelope. The hook still runs; it short-circuits at line 15 only because the bypass token is present. Net: full chmod access inside ~/projects/ except 777/666/000, which require explicit ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 prefix; everything outside ~/projects/ is blocked unless ALLOW_OUTSIDE_WORKSPACE=1 is also prefixed. The settings entry only suppresses the Claude Code prompt — the hooks are the actual fence.
Bash(ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 chmod:*).efficiency_justification: Without this entry, every legitimate hook-bypass chmod (chmod 777 ~/projects/some-asset, chmod 666 fixture-file) triggers a Claude Code permission prompt — defeating the bypass mechanism the operator just typed. Used rarely (handful per quarter when fixing perm-corrupted scripts or test fixtures) but pre-allow keeps the bypass ergonomic. Without it the operator would have to approve the same prompt every session.

Bash(ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 git:*).risk: Pre-allowing the ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 envelope around git could in principle let a malicious or buggy automation execute git reset --hard, git branch -D, git push origin --delete, or git clean (force flag) without the usual Claude Code permission prompt. These commands can discard uncommitted work, delete unmerged branches, remove remote refs, or wipe untracked files.
Bash(ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 git:*).rationale: guard-destructive.sh blocks all four destructive git verbs unless ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 is the operator-typed prefix (lines 19-45). Pre-allowing 'ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 git:*' in settings.json suppresses only the Claude Code permission prompt when the operator has already explicitly typed the bypass token. The hook still runs and short-circuits at line 15 only because ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 is present. Without the operator-typed bypass, the hook still blocks. Net: full git access inside ~/projects/ except the four destructive verbs (reset hard, branch capital-D, push delete, clean force), which require explicit ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 prefix. The settings entry only suppresses the Claude Code prompt — the hook is the actual fence.
Bash(ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 git:*).efficiency_justification: Without this entry, every legitimate hook-bypass git operation (recovering from a botched merge, force-deleting a stale branch, cleaning a polluted worktree) triggers a Claude Code permission prompt — defeating the bypass mechanism the operator just typed. Used several times per quarter (recovery work, branch cleanup) — pre-allow keeps the bypass ergonomic; without it the operator would face redundant approval prompts every session.

Bash(ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 rm:*).risk: Pre-allowing the ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 envelope around rm suppresses the Claude Code permission prompt for rm operations explicitly flagged dangerous by the operator. In principle a malicious or buggy automation could chain rm -rf with the ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 prefix to delete repo files without prompting. The bare Bash(rm:*) entry already allows rm broadly inside the workspace, so the marginal additional risk from this entry is small.
Bash(ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 rm:*).rationale: Two layers of protection: (a) guard-workspace.sh blocks rm with absolute paths outside ~/projects/ regardless of ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE prefix (allowed: workspace, /tmp, /var/folders, /dev/null|stdin|stdout|stderr); (b) guard-destructive.sh does not currently gate rm specifically — the ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE prefix on rm is a vestigial defense-in-depth marker indicating operator intent rather than a real hook bypass. Pre-allowing 'ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 rm:*' suppresses only the Claude Code permission prompt when the operator has typed the intent marker; it does not unlock anything not already covered by Bash(rm:*) plus the workspace fence. Future hardening could add an rm-specific guard in guard-destructive.sh keyed on this prefix; the entry stays in place to support that without re-prompting churn.
Bash(ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 rm:*).efficiency_justification: Without this entry, an operator who types 'ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 rm something-dangerous' to flag intent gets a redundant Claude Code prompt — defeating the intent-marker pattern they just typed. Used rarely, but pre-allow keeps the convention consistent across chmod, git, and rm so the operator's mental model stays uniform: prefix with ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 to flag risky intent, no prompt fires.

Bash(ALLOW_MAIN=1 git:*).risk: Pre-allowing the ALLOW_MAIN=1 envelope around git suppresses the Claude Code permission prompt for direct commits/merges/pushes on main/master. In principle a malicious or buggy automation could chain ALLOW_MAIN=1 with destructive git operations on the protected branch without prompting, bypassing the protect-main.sh fence's user-confirmation step. Affects only branch-targeting semantics — feature-branch work is unaffected.
Bash(ALLOW_MAIN=1 git:*).rationale: Two layers of protection: (a) protect-main.sh blocks any git operation that would commit to or push directly onto main/master unless ALLOW_MAIN=1 is the operator-typed prefix (the hook is the fence; the prefix is the explicit opt-in); (b) the workspace and force-push hooks remain in effect — ALLOW_MAIN=1 doesn't bypass guard-force-push.sh or guard-workspace.sh. Pre-allowing 'ALLOW_MAIN=1 git:*' in settings.json removes the redundant Claude Code prompt when the operator has already typed the bypass token. Used by deliberate boundary commits (session-kind /stasis snapshots, one-line chore commits like the permissions-log entries this skill writes, doc-only commits between features). Net: direct main commits remain gated by protect-main.sh; the settings entry only suppresses the Claude Code prompt when the operator has explicitly opted in.
Bash(ALLOW_MAIN=1 git:*).efficiency_justification: Without this entry, every legitimate boundary commit to main (session stasis snapshot, permissions-log update, chore commit) triggers a Claude Code permission prompt — defeating the intent of the ALLOW_MAIN=1 prefix and turning a deterministic two-keystroke workflow into a multi-step approval dance. Used 5-15 times per quarter (every session boundary, every permissions review, every doc-only chore). Pre-allow keeps the bypass ergonomic; without it the operator faces a prompt on the most routine non-feature commits.

Bash(bash:*).risk: Pre-allowing the broad Bash(bash:*) wildcard means any bash invocation — bash some-script.sh, bash -c 'arbitrary commands', bash -lc, etc. — runs without a Claude Code permission prompt. A malicious or compromised script inside ~/projects/ could be executed silently. The broad pattern also means novel bash invocation forms (newly-introduced scripts, third-party shell tools dropped into the workspace) inherit allow-by-default rather than prompt-on-first-sight.
Bash(bash:*).rationale: Defense-in-depth via guard-workspace.sh: bash is one of six gated file-mutation verbs (rm, cp, mv, chmod, chown, bash). Any bash command containing an absolute path or tilde-prefixed path outside ~/projects/ is blocked unless ALLOW_OUTSIDE_WORKSPACE=1 is the operator-typed prefix. Whitelisted prefixes inside the fence: workspace itself, /tmp/, /var/folders/, /dev/null|stdin|stdout|stderr. Inside the workspace the bash invocation is trusted because the workspace is a controlled environment under operator supervision. Pre-allowing 'bash:*' in settings.json suppresses the Claude Code permission prompt that would otherwise fire on every script call — making ccanvil's deterministic-first workflow (where every routine action is a small bash script via .ccanvil/scripts/, .claude/hooks/, hub/scripts/) practical. The hook layer is the actual fence; the settings entry only suppresses the prompt.
Bash(bash:*).efficiency_justification: This is the foundational substrate for ccanvil's script-heavy workflow. Every /stasis, /recall, /idea, /spec, /plan, /pr, /land, /permissions-review, /radar, /review invocation runs at least one .ccanvil/scripts/* or .claude/hooks/* shell script via bash. Without 'Bash(bash:*)' pre-allowed, every single one of these invocations triggers a Claude Code permission prompt for the specific script form on first sight — easily 50-200 prompts per session early on, dropping over time as exact forms accumulate in settings.local.json (which is the prompt-and-persist drift BTS-150 documents). Pre-allowing the broad form eliminates this churn entirely while the workspace fence keeps the safety boundary intact. Without it the deterministic-first principle is operationally unworkable.

Bash(cat:*).risk: Pre-allowing the broad Bash(cat:*) wildcard means any cat invocation runs without a Claude Code permission prompt — including paths outside ~/projects/. cat is read-only, so the threat model is data exfiltration to Claude context (e.g. cat ~/.aws/credentials, cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa, cat /etc/passwd). The marginal risk over the Read tool's existing capability is small: Read already exposes file contents to Claude without per-path prompts. The structural concern is that the bash route to file contents has no workspace fence today, while the rest of the gated verbs (rm/cp/mv/chmod/chown/bash) do.
Bash(cat:*).rationale: BTS-153 captured to extend guard-workspace.sh's gated verb list to include cat with the same workspace-fence semantics as the existing six verbs (or add a separate read-fence hook). Until BTS-153 lands, this entry pre-allows cat broadly because: (a) cat is read-only and the worst case (Claude reading a sensitive file) is already reachable via the Read tool which has no per-path prompt; (b) cat is foundational shell plumbing used by virtually every script and ad-hoc command — gating it without a hook in place would reintroduce per-form prompt-and-persist drift exactly like the broad bash:* case. When BTS-153 ships, this rationale should be revised to reflect the new fence and the threat model should be re-priced. Net today: full cat access; safety boundary is the Read-tool-equivalent threat model, not a workspace fence.
Bash(cat:*).efficiency_justification: cat is foundational. Every spec/plan/idea operation, every script, every test, every git workflow eventually pipes through cat-equivalent reads. Pre-allowing the broad form prevents per-invocation prompt churn that would otherwise multiply across 50-200 cat invocations per session. Without it the deterministic-first script-heavy workflow becomes operationally noisy. Trade-off accepted for now; BTS-153 is the planned hardening path.

Bash(chmod:*).risk: Pre-allowing the broad Bash(chmod:*) wildcard means any chmod invocation runs without a Claude Code permission prompt. A malicious or buggy automation could change file permissions to harmful states (group-writable on private files, executable bits on data files, etc.). The catastrophic numeric modes (777 world-writable, 666 world-writable, 000 fully locked) are gated by guard-destructive.sh — but the broad pattern leaves all other modes (644, 755, +x, -x, g+w, etc.) freely usable.
Bash(chmod:*).rationale: Two layers of protection: (a) guard-workspace.sh blocks chmod with absolute paths outside ~/projects/ (with /tmp, /var/folders, /dev/null|stdin|stdout|stderr whitelisted); (b) guard-destructive.sh blocks the catastrophic numeric modes 777|666|000 unless ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 is the operator-typed prefix (covered separately by entry 'ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 chmod:*'). Pre-allowing 'chmod:*' in settings.json suppresses the Claude Code permission prompt for all the routine, safe modes (644 for files, 755 for dirs and scripts, +x for new scripts, etc.) that ccanvil's lifecycle scripts emit constantly. The hooks remain the actual fence. Net inside ~/projects/: full chmod access EXCEPT the three catastrophic modes; outside ~/projects/: blocked. The settings entry only suppresses the Claude Code prompt; the hooks gate.
Bash(chmod:*).efficiency_justification: Routine chmod invocations are pervasive across ccanvil's lifecycle: every new hook or script needs +x, every test fixture script needs setting executable, every freshly-written file may need 644 normalization. Pre-allowing the broad form prevents per-invocation prompt churn — easily 5-20 chmods per active development session, fewer on idle days. Without this entry the deterministic-first workflow becomes noisy on script-creation days; with it, the workflow stays smooth while the catastrophic modes remain gated.

Bash(chown:*).risk: Pre-allowing the broad Bash(chown:*) wildcard means any chown invocation runs without a Claude Code permission prompt. chown changes file ownership; in the worst case a malicious or buggy automation could re-own workspace files in ways that confuse subsequent operations or accidentally lock the operator out of their own files. Cross-user chown requires root which Claude doesn't have, so the practical blast radius is limited to the operator's own files. Genuinely rare in practice — chown is not a routine ccanvil operation.
Bash(chown:*).rationale: One layer of protection: guard-workspace.sh treats chown as one of six gated file-mutation verbs (rm, cp, mv, chmod, chown, bash). Any chown command containing absolute paths or tilde-prefixed paths outside ~/projects/ is blocked unless ALLOW_OUTSIDE_WORKSPACE=1 is the operator-typed prefix (workspace, /tmp, /var/folders, /dev/null|stdin|stdout|stderr whitelisted). No guard-destructive.sh coverage for chown — chown lacks the numeric-mode footgun structure that makes chmod 777/666/000 catastrophic. Inside the workspace, chown is bounded by OS permissions (Claude only has the operator's privileges), so the realistic blast radius is the operator's own files. Pre-allowing 'chown:*' in settings.json suppresses the Claude Code permission prompt; the workspace fence remains the actual gate.
Bash(chown:*).efficiency_justification: chown is rare in practice — not a routine ccanvil operation. Pre-allow exists for symmetry with chmod and for the edge cases where chown comes up (fixing ownership after a sudo mishap, normalizing perms after archive extraction, post-restore work). Without the entry, the rare chown invocation triggers a one-time prompt that gets persisted to settings.local.json (BTS-150 prompt-and-persist drift). Pre-allow the broad form to keep settings.local.json clean and let the workspace fence do the gating. Trade-off: minimal efficiency gain per session, real cleanliness gain in the long-term settings ledger.

Bash(cp:*).risk: Pre-allowing the broad Bash(cp:*) wildcard means any cp invocation runs without a Claude Code permission prompt. cp can overwrite files silently (no interactive prompt by default in scripts). A malicious or buggy automation could overwrite an important file with a zero-byte source, vandalize a target with known-good content, or duplicate sensitive files into less-protected locations. The blast radius is bounded by guard-workspace.sh — both source and destination tokens are scanned, and any absolute or tilde-prefixed path outside ~/projects/ blocks unless the operator typed ALLOW_OUTSIDE_WORKSPACE=1.
Bash(cp:*).rationale: One layer of protection: guard-workspace.sh treats cp as one of six gated file-mutation verbs. Both source and destination paths are tokenized and matched against the workspace fence — any absolute path outside ~/projects/ (or the system-temp whitelist /tmp, /var/folders, /dev/null|stdin|stdout|stderr) triggers BLOCKED with the standard ALLOW_OUTSIDE_WORKSPACE=1 bypass envelope. No guard-destructive.sh coverage for cp because cp doesn't have a structural catastrophic-form (no equivalent to chmod 777). Inside the workspace, cp is foundational shell plumbing — ccanvil-sync.sh uses it for hub→node propagation, install scripts use it for templating, test fixtures use it for setup. Pre-allowing 'cp:*' in settings.json suppresses the Claude Code permission prompt; the workspace fence is the actual gate.
Bash(cp:*).efficiency_justification: cp is among the most-invoked shell verbs in ccanvil. Every ccanvil-sync.sh push/pull copies files, every test fixture setup uses cp, every template-based install copies templates into place. Pre-allowing the broad form prevents per-invocation prompt churn that would otherwise multiply across dozens of cp calls per session. Without the entry, cp invocations trigger first-sight prompts that persist to settings.local.json (BTS-150 drift). Pre-allow keeps the deterministic-first script-heavy workflow operationally smooth.

Bash(done).risk: Zero practical risk. `done` is a bash control-flow keyword that terminates for/while/until loops; it is not an executable command and cannot mutate state. The DANGER classification is a false-positive from the BTS-144 classifier's structural pattern matching — the heuristic flags Bash(<bareword>) shapes as broad-wildcards without semantic understanding of bash grammar. The neighboring entries Bash(for:*), Bash(while:*), Bash(if:*), Bash(do:*), Bash(then:*), Bash(else:*), Bash(elif:*), and Bash(fi) all serve the same purpose; the classifier only flags `done` and `fi` because their regex shape (no trailing :*) trips a different alternative.
Bash(done).rationale: The entry is a no-op approval allowing Claude Code to invoke `done` as a tokenized end-of-pipeline marker without prompting. Bash grammar guarantees `done` cannot execute anything; it only terminates a loop block. No hook gates it because there's nothing to gate. BTS-154 has been captured to refine the classifier to recognize the standard bash control-flow keyword set (for, while, until, if, then, else, elif, fi, do, done, case, esac, in, function, select, time) and exclude them from broad-wildcard DANGER classification. When BTS-154 ships, this audit entry should disappear from the DANGER list entirely. Until then, accept-danger is the correct verb to silence the false-positive without removing the legitimate settings entry.
Bash(done).efficiency_justification: The classifier flag was creating noise in the DANGER review with no actionable signal — `done` cannot be made safer. Pre-allowing the keyword is necessary because Claude Code's tokenizer treats `done` as a bare command when it appears at end-of-pipeline in multi-line shell scripts. Without the entry, every multi-line for/while loop in a Bash tool call would trigger a permission prompt on the closing keyword. Cost of accept-danger: ~30 seconds; cost of skipping: every future audit re-surfaces the false-positive until BTS-154 ships.

Bash(echo:*).risk: Near-zero on its own. echo is a print-only builtin that writes to stdout/stderr — it has no filesystem mutation capability. The only indirect mutation vector is shell redirection (`echo X > file`, `echo X >> file`) where bash parses the redirect operator and writes the file. The redirect side of that pipeline is bash behavior, not echo behavior. A malicious or buggy automation could in principle use echo+redirect to overwrite or append to files inside the workspace; this is constrained by the same boundaries that govern any file write (workspace fence applies to absolute paths in the redirect target, though guard-workspace.sh's regex specifically gates verbs like rm/cp/mv/chmod/chown/bash, not arbitrary redirect targets). Worst case: echo content lands in a tracked file, which then surfaces in `git diff` and remains user-reviewable before any commit.
Bash(echo:*).rationale: echo itself is non-mutating. The settings entry only suppresses the Claude Code permission prompt for echo invocations. No hook needs to gate echo because there is nothing to gate at the echo verb level. Where echo is used in mutation pipelines (echo > file, echo >> file), the safety surface is the redirect target — which surfaces in git diff for any tracked file, and which is bounded by the operator's filesystem permissions for any local file. Pre-allowing 'echo:*' in settings.json is the minimal-friction path for a non-mutating verb that is foundational to shell scripting. No BTS follow-up needed; this is correctly DANGER-by-classifier-pattern but accept-by-semantics.
Bash(echo:*).efficiency_justification: echo is among the most-invoked shell verbs anywhere — every script uses it for status messages, progress logging, structured output assembly, and inline-content generation in heredocs and pipelines. Pre-allowing the broad form prevents prompt churn that would multiply across hundreds of echo invocations per session. Without it the deterministic-first script-heavy workflow becomes operationally untenable. Trade-off: trivially accepted because echo's mutation capability is zero on its own.

Bash(env:*).risk: Two threat vectors. (a) Direct: `env` alone prints environment variables to stdout, exposing them to Claude's context. Exfiltration risk if any credentials live in env vars (uncommon in this setup; secrets are typically in ~/.aws/credentials or ~/.ssh/, not exported). (b) Indirect: `env [VAR=value ...] CMD args` runs CMD with a modified environment, which could be a wrapping vector for command forms the workspace-fence regex doesn't recognize. The hook gates by regex-matching the full command string against tokenized verbs, so `env rm /etc/foo` still scans /etc/foo and blocks. But novel envelope forms `env -i CMD` or PATH manipulation could in principle confuse the regex. Practical blast radius is small because (a) env vars rarely carry secrets here and (b) the workspace fence operates on path tokens regardless of which verb wraps them.
Bash(env:*).rationale: One layer of indirect protection: guard-workspace.sh scans the full command string for path tokens, so any env-wrapped command containing absolute paths outside the workspace still triggers the fence. The wrapping verb (env) doesn't change which tokens get scanned. The settings entry only suppresses the Claude Code permission prompt for env invocations themselves. The env-as-print case (exfiltration) parallels what any tool that reads process environment can already do; it does not introduce new exfiltration capability beyond what's already implicit in Claude's tool access. The env-as-prefix case is bounded by the same hooks that would gate the wrapped command directly — env doesn't bypass the fence, it just changes the environment passed to the child. Net: pre-allowing 'env:*' is safe given the existing fence design; the rare novel wrapping form would still be subject to per-token path scanning.
Bash(env:*).efficiency_justification: env is used by ccanvil scripts in two patterns: (a) prefix form (CCANVIL_DEBUG=1 cmd, ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 cmd) which is technically Bash builtin VAR=val cmd syntax not env, so this is mostly the env-print form; (b) `env -i` clean-environment isolation in test fixtures. Used moderately (5-10 invocations per active session). Pre-allow keeps the deterministic-first workflow smooth without per-form prompt-and-persist drift.

Bash(find:*).risk: Material and currently unmitigated. find is a traversal/read tool that is not in guard-workspace.sh's gated-verb list (rm, cp, mv, chmod, chown, bash) — but find has two embedded mutation operators that bypass the existing leading-verb regex: (a) `find <path> -delete` recursively deletes matching paths with no leading mutation verb, so the fence regex doesn't match. (b) `find <path> -exec rm {} +` (or similar with chmod/mv/chown/bash) runs a destructive child verb as a find argument; the leading-verb regex doesn't see it because the rm/chmod/etc. is not at start-of-command and not preceded by whitespace-as-separator. Threat scenarios: (1) in-workspace `find . -type f -delete` would wipe the entire workspace silently — fence allows it because no leading destructive verb and no out-of-workspace path token. (2) `find . -type f -exec chmod 777 {} +` would happen to trigger guard-destructive.sh's numeric-mode regex, but other destructive -exec forms (rm -rf with embedded args, mv into garbage, chmod 644 on scripts) bypass entirely. (3) Out-of-workspace traversal `find /Library` is covered today only because the path-token scan is verb-agnostic — it catches the absolute path /Library regardless of which verb wraps it. (4) Variable indirection (find $TARGET) bypasses the static tokenizer when $TARGET expands to an out-of-workspace path at runtime.
Bash(find:*).rationale: BTS-155 has been captured at Urgent priority to extend guard-workspace.sh (or add a sibling guard-find.sh) to: (1) add `find` to the gated-verb regex; (2) when find is the leading verb, scan the rest of the command for mutation operators (-delete, -exec with destructive child verbs, -execdir, -okdir) and block unless ALLOW_FIND_MUTATE=1 or ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 is the operator-typed prefix; (3) continue scanning all path tokens via the existing fence (already covers /etc, ~/Library, etc.); (4) document the variable-indirection limitation explicitly, same caveat as the existing rm/cp/mv set. Until BTS-155 ships, this entry pre-allows find broadly because: (a) find is foundational for traversal, search, and conditional file ops — used by ccanvil-sync.sh, audit scripts, test harnesses; (b) the path-token scanner does cover the most common out-of-workspace form (`find /etc ...`) verb-agnostically; (c) the in-workspace mutation case is the gap, and accepting it as a known unmitigated risk pending BTS-155 is more honest than skipping the entry and letting it re-surface noisily without action. When BTS-155 ships, this rationale should be revised to reflect the new fence semantics.
Bash(find:*).efficiency_justification: find is among the most-invoked traversal verbs across ccanvil's automation: ccanvil-sync.sh enumerates preset files, audit scripts traverse the project for stale references, test harnesses locate fixtures, lifecycle scripts find archived specs. Pre-allowing the broad form prevents prompt churn that would multiply across dozens of find invocations per session. Without the entry, every novel find form triggers a first-sight prompt that persists to settings.local.json (BTS-150 drift). Trade-off accepted now; BTS-155 is the planned hardening path that will let this entry stay in settings.json with its threat surface bounded by the new fence.

Bash(mv:*).risk: Pre-allowing the broad Bash(mv:*) wildcard means any mv invocation runs without a Claude Code permission prompt. mv overwrites destination files silently (no interactive prompt by default in scripts). A malicious or buggy automation could overwrite an important file by moving over it, vandalize a target with known-good content, or leak files by moving them into a less-protected location. mv across filesystem boundaries is effectively cp+rm, so the threat parity is with both verbs combined.
Bash(mv:*).rationale: Two-layer defense plus a structural recovery argument. (a) guard-workspace.sh treats mv as one of six gated file-mutation verbs — both source and destination paths are tokenized and matched against the workspace fence. Any absolute or tilde-prefixed path outside ~/projects/ (or the system-temp whitelist /tmp, /var/folders, /dev/null|stdin|stdout|stderr) triggers BLOCKED with the standard ALLOW_OUTSIDE_WORKSPACE=1 bypass envelope. (b) No guard-destructive.sh coverage for mv because mv lacks a structural catastrophic-form (no equivalent to chmod 777). (c) Recovery argument: most files inside ccanvil projects are tracked by git. Even if an mv overwrites or relocates a tracked file inadvertently, the prior content is recoverable via git checkout, git stash, git reflog, or git restore. The blast radius for tracked files is bounded by 'inconvenience to recover' rather than 'permanent loss.' Untracked files (.ccanvil/ideas-pending.log, .claude/settings.local.json, /tmp/* artifacts) are the genuine loss surface — but these are either gitignored-by-design (regenerable state) or tmp-by-design (ephemeral). Pre-allowing 'mv:*' in settings.json suppresses the Claude Code permission prompt; the workspace fence is the actual gate, and git is the recovery substrate.
Bash(mv:*).efficiency_justification: mv is foundational for atomic-write patterns: write to tmpfile, then mv tmpfile into place. Used by virtually every script that mutates a tracked file safely (permissions-audit.sh apply, ccanvil-sync.sh propagation, idea-update, spec/plan archive transitions). Pre-allowing the broad form prevents per-invocation prompt churn — easily 10-30 mvs per active development session. Without it the deterministic-first script-heavy workflow becomes noisy, and atomic-write idioms become user-confirmation-coupled rather than transparent.

Bash(rm:*).risk: Material and currently unmitigated for the recursive-force shape. rm has zero coverage in guard-destructive.sh today — the only rm gating is guard-workspace.sh's path-fence, which blocks rm with absolute or tilde-prefixed paths outside ~/projects/ (with the system-temp whitelist). Inside the workspace, every rm variant including recursive-force flag combinations runs without prompt or block. Threat scenarios: (1) In-workspace recursive wipe via rm -rf at the project root or via rm -rf $(find . -type d) — wipes the workspace silently. Tracked files recoverable via git, but uncommitted work, gitignored regenerable state (settings.local.json, ideas-pending.log, registry.json), and in-flight changes are lost. (2) Tmp-dir wipe via rm -rf /tmp/some-prefix-* runs without prompt because /tmp is whitelisted by the workspace fence — easy footgun if the prefix is wrong. (3) Recursive over symlinks: rm -rf follows symlinks, so a symlink inside the workspace pointing outside could route the recursion across the fence. The static path-token scan does not follow symlinks.
Bash(rm:*).rationale: BTS-156 has been captured at Urgent priority to extend guard-destructive.sh with an rm-recursive-force regex sibling to the existing chmod-numeric-mode block. The proposed gate matches any rm invocation with both recursive (-r/-R/--recursive) AND force (-f/--force) flags in any combination, blocks by default, and accepts the standard ALLOW_DESTRUCTIVE=1 operator-typed prefix as the bypass envelope. Path-agnostic by design — the threat is the recursive-force shape, not the path location. Until BTS-156 ships, this entry pre-allows rm broadly because: (a) rm is foundational for cleanup, archive transitions, and tmp-file lifecycle in dozens of ccanvil scripts; (b) the workspace fence does cover out-of-workspace paths verb-agnostically (rm /etc/foo blocks); (c) the in-workspace recursive-force gap is the acute case, accepted as a known unmitigated risk pending BTS-156 with the explicit understanding that this rationale must be revised when the gate ships. The git-tracked recovery substrate provides a partial mitigation for tracked files (recoverable via reflog/checkout/restore) but not for untracked workspace state. Decision was made with eyes open: BTS-156 is high-priority follow-up and this entry will be re-reviewed once the gate is in place.
Bash(rm:*).efficiency_justification: rm is foundational across ccanvil's automation: every cleanup pass, archive transition, /tmp lifecycle, and atomic-write pattern (write-tmp + mv-into-place + rm-on-failure) uses rm. Pre-allowing the broad form prevents prompt churn that would multiply across dozens of rm invocations per active session. Without the entry, every novel rm form triggers a first-sight prompt that persists to settings.local.json (BTS-150 drift) — and the prompt-and-persist drift on rm specifically would multiply rapidly because rm's argument shapes vary widely (single file, multi-file, glob, dir trailing slash, etc.). Trade-off accepted now; BTS-156 is the planned hardening that will close the recursive-force gap while preserving non-recursive ergonomics.

Bash(sort:*).risk: Small but real. sort is a read-only text-processing utility in its primary form (read input, sort lines, write to stdout). The mutation vector is the `-o FILE` flag, which overwrites FILE with the sorted output. The workspace fence (guard-workspace.sh) is verb-leading — it gates rm, cp, mv, chmod, chown, bash — and sort is not in that list. So `sort -o ~/.zshrc input` would write to ~/.zshrc with no current hook intercept. Threat is bounded by (a) the explicit -o flag requirement (no accidental writes via sort without -o) and (b) operator filesystem permissions, but the gap is the same anti-pattern as BTS-155 (find) and BTS-156 (rm -rf): tools that take file destinations as flag arguments bypass the verb-leading fence regex. Worst case: a malicious or buggy automation uses sort -o to overwrite a sensitive config file (~/.zshrc, ~/.gitconfig, ~/.aws/credentials) silently.
Bash(sort:*).rationale: BTS-157 has been captured at Urgent priority to gate sort -o (and the broader text-utility output-flag family — sed -i, tee FILE, awk redirect, uniq -o, and bash-level > and >> redirection) under the same workspace-fence semantics as the verb-leading set. Two paths proposed: narrow (sort -o specifically) and broad (general destination-flag fence covering the entire family). Path B is structurally correct; Path A is the stopgap. Until BTS-157 ships, this entry pre-allows sort broadly because: (a) sort is foundational text-processing — used by audit scripts, log analysis, dedup pipelines, ccanvil's deterministic-first scripts; (b) the read-only stdout form (sort input, sort -u input, sort -k 2 input) is the overwhelmingly common case and entirely safe; (c) the -o-flag mutation vector is rare in ccanvil's actual usage and bounded by operator permissions. Accept-danger is the right verb for now: the gap is real, captured, and pending hardening — silencing the audit while BTS-157 is on the urgent queue avoids re-prompt churn without losing the threat awareness.
Bash(sort:*).efficiency_justification: sort is foundational text processing. Used by audit scripts, log analysis, dedup pipelines, idea-list rendering, spec-list ordering, permissions-audit's category breakdowns. Pre-allowing the broad form prevents prompt churn that would multiply across dozens of sort invocations per active session. Without the entry, every novel sort form (different flag combos: -u, -r, -n, -k, -t, etc.) triggers a first-sight prompt that persists to settings.local.json (BTS-150 drift) — and sort's argument shapes vary widely, so the drift would multiply rapidly. Trade-off accepted now; BTS-157 is the planned hardening.
